summary: John Dewey was a good friend of mine for over sixty years. We shared many intellectual discussions, and his ideas greatly influenced my thinking throughout my academic career. Our friendship was a significant part of my intellectual life, especially during my time at the University of California.

I was called to the University of California, Berkeley to teach Latin, which was an exciting opportunity to be part of a growing university. I also created and ran the University of California Extension for over fifteen years, developing curricula that served diverse groups including immigrants, laborers, and prisoners. My work there included coordinating with the military bureau during World War I and maintaining good relations between Extension and the faculty.

I played a key role in transforming the Berkeley Public Library from a tiny storefront into a grand structure financed by Carnegie funds. This effort ensured the library could serve the community well and grow with the needs of Berkeley, California. It was important to me that the library be a strong cultural and educational resource for the city.

The Carnegie Corporation provided the funds that made it possible to build the grand Berkeley Public Library, which I helped develop. Their support was crucial in expanding the library beyond its humble beginnings, allowing it to better serve the Berkeley community. I was deeply involved in securing and utilizing these funds effectively.

During World War I, I handled the liaison agency between Washington, D.C. and the University of California military bureau. This role involved coordinating efforts to support the war, linking the university's resources with federal needs. It was a critical period where academic and military efforts intersected.

Washington, D.C. was central to my work during World War I, where I served as a liaison between the city and the University of California's military bureau. This connection was vital for coordinating military and academic collaboration during the war. My role required frequent communication and organization between these two important centers.

I created and ran the University of California Extension for over fifteen years, developing curricula that served a wide range of people including immigrants, laborers, and prisoners. I also started the Department of Labor Education within Extension, focusing on education for workers and addressing issues like communism and labor rights. My goal was to make education accessible and relevant beyond the traditional university setting.

I headed the relief committee during the disastrous Berkeley Fire of 1923, helping to coordinate aid and recovery efforts for the community. This was a challenging time for Berkeley, California, and I took an active role in organizing support to help residents rebuild. The experience deepened my commitment to public service in the city.

Berkeley, California has been my home and the center of much of my work, from teaching at the University of California to developing the public library and leading fire relief efforts. I was also involved in local politics, running as a moderate mayoral candidate in 1945. The city has been both my professional and personal community for many decades.

entity: Philological Association of the Pacific Coast
summary: I was an instigator in founding the Philological Association of the Pacific Coast, aiming to support the study and appreciation of languages and philology. This organization reflected my lifelong commitment to classical languages and education. It was part of my broader effort to keep classical studies vibrant despite changing academic trends.

I helped establish the American Association for Adult Education to promote lifelong learning and educational opportunities for adults. This aligned with my work in University Extension and my belief in education as a tool for democracy and social progress. The association was part of my efforts to expand education beyond traditional students.

I was instrumental in founding the University of California Faculty Club, which provided a social and intellectual gathering place for faculty members. This helped foster community and collaboration among university staff. It was one of several organizations I helped create to strengthen academic life at Berkeley.

I played a key role in establishing the English-Speaking Union, an organization dedicated to promoting international understanding through the English language. This reflected my interest in language education and cultural exchange. The union was part of my broader commitment to education and communication.

Through University Extension, we developed educational programs for prisoners, including those at Alcatraz Federal Penitentiary. Our goal was to provide meaningful learning opportunities even within the prison system. This work was part of my broader commitment to education for all, regardless of circumstance.

We also extended educational programs to inmates at San Quentin State Prison, aiming to offer them valuable learning experiences. This was part of the University of California Extension's efforts to reach diverse populations, including prisoners. I believed strongly in the power of education to transform lives, even behind bars.
